attributes.stem: A 28-year-old woman is admitted to the labour ward at 40 weeks gestation with her first pregnancy. She has been in active labour for 12 hours and has been fully dilated for two hours. She has no significant medical history and has a dense epidural in situ. On examination, she appears exhausted. Her pulse rate is 100/min, blood pressure 130/85 mmHg, respiratory rate 20/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 37.2°C. Vaginal examination reveals the fetal head is at +2 station with the occiput in the anterior position. The cardiotocograph (CTG) shows fetal bradycardia at 80 beats per minute for the past five minutes.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
attributes.explanation:
This scenario presents a primigravida woman in prolonged second stage of labour with acute fetal bradycardia requiring urgent delivery. The most appropriate next step in management is forceps-assisted vaginal delivery.

Forceps-assisted vaginal delivery is indicated in this case due to several factors. The patient has been fully dilated for two hours, which is approaching the upper limit of normal for a nulliparous woman. More critically, the CTG shows fetal bradycardia for five minutes, indicating severe fetal distress and the need for immediate delivery. The fetal head position (occiput anterior) and station (+2) are favourable for forceps application. Additionally, the presence of a dense epidural favours forceps over vacuum, as forceps allow for more controlled traction in a patient with reduced ability to push.

Immediate caesarean section would be an option for fetal bradycardia if the head station was higher or the cervix was not fully dilated. It is likely in this case that vaginal delivery with instrument assistance will achieve a faster and safer delivery for mother and baby. Fully dilated Caesarean section deliveries can also be extremely challenging due to the low fetal head and risk injuries for mothers and babies.

Continue expectant management is not appropriate given the acute fetal bradycardia. Any delay in intervention could lead to severe fetal compromise or death.

Vacuum-assisted vaginal delivery is an alternative method of operative vaginal delivery. However, forceps are generally preferred when there is a need for immediate delivery and when the mother has a dense epidural, as they allow for more rapid and controlled extraction compared to vacuum.

Oxytocin augmentation is absolutely contraindicated in this scenario. With acute fetal bradycardia, increasing uterine contractions would further compromise fetal oxygenation and well-being.

It's important to note that forceps delivery should only be attempted by trained professionals. Continuous fetal heart rate monitoring should be maintained throughout the procedure, and preparations should be made for an emergency caesarean section if forceps delivery is unsuccessful. The neonatal team should also be alerted due to the likelihood of requiring resuscitation given the prolonged fetal bradycardia.
learningPoints: Forceps delivery is indicated when there is a need for immediate delivery in the second stage of labour, especially with fetal bradycardia.
Fetal head position and station are crucial factors in determining the appropriateness of forceps delivery.
A dense epidural may favour forceps over vacuum-assisted delivery due to the need for more traction.
references: Murphy DJ, Strachan BK, Bahl R, on behalf of the Royal College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists. Assisted Vaginal Birth. Green-top Guideline No. 26. BJOG. 2020 Apr 28. Available from: https://doi.org/10.1111/1471-0528.16092
